topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, instantly stealing the air from my lungs. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly before I managed to open it. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party scheduled for Saturday has been cancelled." The words swam before my eyes, blurring the brightly colored decorations I’d been mentally planning for weeks. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like a creeping vine. I reread the message, half-expecting a typo, a cruel joke. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my living room, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. My breath came in short, shallow gasps. I sank onto the sofa, burying my face in my hands, the sound of my own shaky exhale the loudest noise in the room.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the notification blinking mockingly. It was impossible. I reread the cancellation notice three times, my brow furrowed in disbelief. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I felt a sudden, intense pressure building behind my eyes, a tightness that made focusing on anything else excruciating. I pushed back from my desk, knocking over a stack of papers with a clatter that seemed deafening in the quiet office. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent cityscape, feeling a profound sense of deflation wash over me. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles in my neck strain.

[story 3]

When the message finally registered, a strange, hollow emptiness settled in my chest. I didn't move for a long moment, just sat frozen, the phone still clutched in my hand. The initial shock morphed into a heavy, leaden feeling. I let out a slow, ragged sigh, the sound weak and unused. My hands started to shake uncontrollably, and I had to press them against my thighs to steady them. Every nerve ending felt exposed, screaming a silent alarm. I stood up, moving mechanically toward the kitchen, needing something solid to focus on, anything to anchor the swirling uncertainty inside my head.
